question: What are the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming? Explain each briefly.
idealAnswer: 1) Encapsulation: Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a class, hiding internal details. Access modifiers (private, protected, public) control visibility. 2) Inheritance: A class (child) can inherit properties and methods from another class (parent). Enables code reuse. 'is-a' relationship. 3) Polymorphism: Same interface, different implementations. Compile-time (function overloading) and runtime (virtual functions/method overriding). 4) Abstraction: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only the necessary interface. Achieved via abstract classes and interfaces.

question: Explain the difference between 'is-a' and 'has-a' relationships in OOP with examples.
idealAnswer: 'is-a' represents inheritance — a child class IS A type of parent class. Example: Dog is-a Animal, Car is-a Vehicle. Implemented with class Dog : public Animal. 'has-a' represents composition — a class HAS A member of another class. Example: Car has-a Engine, University has-a Department. Implemented by having an Engine object as a member of Car class. Prefer composition over inheritance when possible because it provides better flexibility, loose coupling, and avoids the fragile base class problem. Inheritance creates tight coupling.

question: Write a C++ example demonstrating runtime polymorphism using virtual functions.
hints: Use a base class pointer to call derived class methods
keyPoints: base class pointer calls derived method

question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface? When would you use each?
idealAnswer: Abstract class: Can have both abstract (pure virtual) and concrete methods. Can have member variables and constructors. Supports access modifiers. A class can inherit only one abstract class (in C++/Java). Use when: classes share common behavior AND state. Interface: Only method signatures (pure virtual in C++, interface in Java). No member variables (except constants). All methods are implicitly public. A class can implement multiple interfaces. Use when: unrelated classes need to follow the same contract. Example: Animal abstract class (has name, age + abstract makeSound()). Printable interface (has print() method — any class can be Printable).
keyPoints: abstract class can have concrete methods

question: What is encapsulation? How is it implemented in C++? Why is it important?
idealAnswer: Encapsulation bundles data (member variables) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), restricting direct access to internal data. Implemented using access modifiers: private (accessible only within class), protected (within class and derived classes), public (accessible everywhere). Getters and setters provide controlled access to private data. Importance: 1) Data protection — prevents invalid states. 2) Implementation hiding — can change internals without affecting users. 3) Maintainability — changes are localized. 4) Validation — setters can validate input before setting.

question: Explain constructors and destructors in C++. What are the different types of constructors?
idealAnswer: Constructor: Special method called automatically when an object is created. Same name as class, no return type. Types: 1) Default constructor — no parameters: MyClass() {}. 2) Parameterized constructor — takes arguments: MyClass(int x) { this->x = x; }. 3) Copy constructor — creates object from another: MyClass(const MyClass& other). Called when passing by value or initializing from another object. Destructor: Called automatically when object goes out of scope or is deleted. Name: ~MyClass(). Used for cleanup (freeing memory, closing files). No parameters, no return type, only one per class. Order: Constructors called in creation order, destructors in reverse order.

question: Explain the SOLID principles in OOP. You can briefly describe each.
idealAnswer: S — Single Responsibility: A class should have only one reason to change. Example: Don't mix database logic and UI logic in one class. O — Open/Closed: Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. Use inheritance/interfaces to add behavior without changing existing code. L — Liskov Substitution: Derived classes should be substitutable for their base classes without breaking functionality. If Square extends Rectangle, it should work wherever Rectangle is used. I — Interface Segregation: Don't force classes to implement interfaces they don't use. Many specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface. D — Dependency Inversion: High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Use interfaces for dependencies.

hints: Constructor order: parent first. Destructor order: child first.
idealAnswer:
Output:
Base Constructor
Derived Constructor
Derived Destructor
Base Destructor

Constructors are called from base to derived (parent first, then child). Destructors are called in reverse order (child first, then parent). This ensures the base part is fully constructed before the derived part uses it, and the derived part is cleaned up before the base part is destroyed.
keyPoints: output is Base Derived Derived Base

question: What is the difference between function overloading and function overriding in C++?
idealAnswer: Function Overloading (Compile-time polymorphism): Same function name but different parameter lists (number, type, or order). Resolved at compile time by the compiler. Can happen in the same class. Example: int add(int a, int b) and float add(float a, float b). Function Overriding (Runtime polymorphism): Derived class provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in the base class. Same name, same parameters, same return type. Requires virtual keyword in base class. Resolved at runtime via vtable. Must happen across inheritance hierarchy. Example: Base::draw() overridden by Derived::draw().
keyPoints: overriding = same signature in derived class

question: Explain the Singleton design pattern. When would you use it? What are its drawbacks?
What is the 'static' keyword in C++? Explain static variables and static methods.
idealAnswer: Static Variable: Inside a function — retains value between calls (not destroyed on function return). Inside a class — shared by all objects (one copy for all instances). Exists even without creating an object. Static Method: Belongs to the class, not to any object. Can be called using ClassName::method(). Can only access static members (not instance variables). Cannot use 'this' pointer. Use case: Utility functions, counters shared across instances, factory methods. Example: Math::sqrt(), a counter tracking total objects created.
keyPoints: static var shared by all objects
use for utilities and shared data

question: Compare procedural programming and object-oriented programming. Give advantages of OOP.
idealAnswer: Procedural: Program is a sequence of functions operating on data. Data and functions are separate. Top-down approach. Examples: C, Pascal. OOP: Program is a collection of objects that contain both data and methods. Bottom-up approach. Examples: C++, Java, Python. Advantages of OOP: 1) Modularity — code organized into classes. 2) Reusability — inheritance enables code reuse. 3) Maintainability — encapsulation localizes changes. 4) Flexibility — polymorphism allows different behaviors. 5) Real-world modeling — objects map to real entities. Disadvantages of OOP: Steeper learning curve, can be overkill for simple programs, potential overhead.

question: Write a C++ abstract class 'Vehicle' with pure virtual functions start() and stop(). Create a 'Car' class that inherits from it and implements these functions.
keyPoints: derived must implement all pure virtual

question: In C++, class A has a private member x, protected member y, and public member z. Class B inherits publicly from A. What can B access? What can an object of B access?
idealAnswer:
Inside class B (member functions): Can access y (protected) and z (public) from A. CANNOT access x (private) — private members are never accessible by derived classes. Outside (object of B): Can only access z (public). Cannot access x (private) or y (protected) — protected is accessible within the class hierarchy only, not from outside. Summary table:
- Private x: A only
- Protected y: A and B (inside), not outside
- Public z: A, B, and outside
Protected inheritance would make y and z protected in B. Private inheritance would make both private in B.

question: What is a virtual destructor? Why is it important when using polymorphism?
hints: What happens when you delete a derived object through a base pointer?
idealAnswer: A virtual destructor ensures that when a derived class object is deleted through a base class pointer, the derived class destructor is called first, followed by the base class destructor. Without virtual destructor: Base* p = new Derived(); delete p; — only Base's destructor runs, Derived's destructor is skipped. This causes resource leaks if Derived allocated memory or opened files. With virtual destructor: Both destructors run correctly (Derived first, then Base). Rule: If a class has ANY virtual function, it should have a virtual destructor. The destructor is virtual to ensure proper cleanup through polymorphic pointers.

question: What are templates in C++? Show a simple example of a function template.
Explain the Factory Method design pattern briefly. Give a use case.
idealAnswer: Factory Method defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Instead of calling 'new ClassName()' directly, you call a factory method that returns the appropriate object. Example: A LoggerFactory with createLogger() method. Based on configuration, it returns FileLogger, ConsoleLogger, or DatabaseLogger — all implementing a Logger interface. The client code only knows about Logger, not the specific implementations. Benefits: 1) Decouples object creation from usage. 2) Easy to add new types without changing client code. 3) Follows Open/Closed Principle. Used in: Java's Calendar.getInstance(), document editors (createDocument returns different doc types).

question: What is the 'this' pointer in C++? When is it used?
idealAnswer: 'this' is an implicit pointer available inside non-static member functions that points to the current object (the object on which the method was called). Uses: 1) Disambiguate member variables from parameters with the same name: this->x = x; 2) Return current object for method chaining: return *this; 3) Pass current object to another function: someFunction(this); 4) Compare object identity: if (this == &other). 'this' is a constant pointer — you can't change what it points to (this = something is invalid). Static methods don't have 'this' because they belong to the class, not an instance.

question: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance? How does C++ solve it?
idealAnswer: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base class. Example: class Animal; class Mammal : Animal; class WingedAnimal : Animal; class Bat : Mammal, WingedAnimal. Bat has TWO copies of Animal's members — ambiguity about which copy to use. C++ solves this with virtual inheritance: class Mammal : virtual public Animal; class WingedAnimal : virtual public Animal; Now Bat gets only ONE copy of Animal. The 'virtual' keyword ensures the common base class is shared, not duplicated. The most derived class (Bat) is responsible for constructing the virtual base class.

question: Write a C++ class 'Complex' that overloads the + operator to add two complex numbers.
keyPoints: const method since it doesn't modify

question: What is a Class and what is an Object in OOP?
idealAnswer: Class: A blueprint or template that defines the structure, variables (attributes), and methods (functions) common to all objects of a certain kind. It does not occupy memory space. Example: 'Car' is a class. Object: An instance of a class. It is a real-world entity that has a state (defined by its attributes) and behavior (defined by methods). It occupies memory. Example: 'My Red Toyota' is an object of the Car class.
keyPoints: Object is an instance holding state
Class doesn't take memory, Object does

question: Why is OOP needed? What problems does it solve?
idealAnswer: Before OOP, procedural programming resulted in scattered data and functions ('spaghetti code'), making large software hard to maintain. OOP solves this by: 1) Modularity: Code represents real-world entities, making it understandable. 2) Encapsulation: Data is protected from unintended modification. 3) Reusability: Using inheritance, we write code once and reuse it. 4) Maintainability: Changes in a class don't break the whole program. OOP provides structure for building scalable, enterprise-level systems seamlessly.

question: What are the drawbacks of OOP?
idealAnswer: 1) Complexity: OOP can over-engineer simple problems (defining classes vs just writing a function). 2) Performance Overhead: Creating objects, virtual method dispatching, and abstraction layers cost memory and CPU time compared to low-level procedural code. 3) Steep Learning Curve: Harder to master patterns, abstract concepts, design rules. 4) State Management: Mutable objects traversing the system can cause bugs that are hard to trace, which functional programming avoids.

question: Explain exactly why Overloading is Compile-time and Overriding is Runtime polymorphism.
idealAnswer: Overloading (Compile-Time): When we have two methods with the same name but different parameters, the Compiler determines strictly which method to call based on the arguments passed while compiling the code. Overriding (Runtime): A child class provides a specific implementation of a parent's virtual method. The compiler does not know the exact type of object a Base pointer holds until the program runs. At runtime, the program looks at the virtual table (vtable) of the actual object in memory to invoke the overridden method.
keyPoints: Overloading matches params at compile time
Overriding uses vtable at runtime based on actual object type

question: Explain Association, Aggregation, and Composition with real-world examples.
idealAnswer: Association: A general relationship where two classes use each other. Example: Payment processing uses a User. Both can exist independently. Aggregation: A "has-a" relationship where the child CAN exist independently of the parent (weak association). Example: University has Departments. Departments can exist if University is closed? Or better: Department has Teachers. If the Department closes, Teachers still exist. Composition: A strictly "has-a" relationship where the child CANNOT exist independently of the parent (strong association). Example: Car has an Engine. Or House has Rooms. If House is destroyed, Rooms are destroyed.

question: What is the DRY principle? Give an example.
idealAnswer: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself. Every piece of logic or data should have a single, unambiguous representation within a system. Example: Instead of writing password length and character validation rules inside both the Registration function and the Change Password function, extract it into a validatePassword() utility function and call it from both places. If rules change, you only update it once.

question: What is the YAGNI principle? Give an example.
idealAnswer: YAGNI stands for You Aren't Gonna Need It. It means developers should not add functionality until deemed strictly necessary. Example: We only need to support bKash payments currently. A developer builds a massive abstract payment engine supporting 15 international gateways 'just in case we need it later'. YAGNI says: build only the bKash integration cleanly. Unused features bloat the codebase, cause bugs, and waste time.

question: What is the KISS principle?
idealAnswer: KISS stands for Keep It Simple, Stupid. It states that most systems work best if they are kept simple rather than made complex. Simplicity should be a key goal in design. Avoid overly clever architecture, deeply nested inheritance trees, and over-abstracted patterns when a straightforward solution works and is easier for the team to read and maintain.

question: Explain the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) with an example.
idealAnswer: SRP states that a class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should only have one job. Bad Example: An Employee class calculates salary, saves the employee to the database, and prints a report. If the DB changes, we change the class; if salary logic changes, we change the class. Good Example: Create three classes: Employee (holds data), EmployeeRepository (DB saves), and EmployeeReportRenderer (printing). Now each class has exactly 1 reason to change.

question: Explain the Open/Closed Principle (OCP) with an example.
idealAnswer: OCP states that software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. We should be able to add new functionality without altering existing code. Example: A discount calculator checks if user == 'VIP' do X, if 'Regular' do Y. To add 'Premium', we must modify the central class. Solution: Create a DiscountStrategy interface with calculate() method. Make VIPDiscount and RegularDiscount classes. To add Premium, just create PremiumDiscount implementing the interface. The central calculator code never changes!
keyPoints: Open for extension, closed for modification
Never touch core tested business logic

question: Explain the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP). Give an example of violating it.
idealAnswer: LSP states that objects of a child class must be able to replace objects of the parent class without breaking the application. Violation Example: Parent class Bird has fly() method. Child class Penguin inherits Bird, but throws an Exception or does nothing inside fly(). If a system loop expects a list of Birds and calls fly(), the Penguin breaks it! Fix: Create an interface FlyableBird and NonFlyableBird, separating behaviors so children actually act like their parents.
keyPoints: Child must behave fully like parent
Penguin inheriting flying Bird is a violation

question: Explain the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) with an example.
idealAnswer: ISP states that classes should not be forced to implement interfaces they do not use. Bad Example: An IMachine interface has print(), scan(), and fax(). A SimplePrinter class is forced to implement scan() and fax() just to throw "Not Implemented" errors. Good Example: Segregate into IPrinter, IScanner, IFaxer. ComplexMachine implements all three, SimplePrinter implements only IPrinter.
keyPoints: Split into multiple small, specific interfaces

question: Explain the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) with an example.
idealAnswer: DIP states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions (interfaces). Bad Example: A PasswordReminder class instantiates a MySQLDatabase internally. Hard-coupled. Good Example: Create an IDatabase interface. Inject IDatabase into PasswordReminder via constructor (Dependency Injection). Now you can easily swap MySQLDatabase with MongoDatabase without touching the PasswordReminder code.

question: Explain the Factory pattern. Give an example.
idealAnswer: Factory is a creational pattern that provides an interface for creating objects in a superclass, but allows subclasses to alter the type of objects that will be created. Example: A PaymentProcessorFactory taking a string 'bkash'. It handles the logic to instantiate and return a BkashPayment object, hiding the instantiation complexity from the client code.
keyPoints: Creator class returns subclasses based on inputs

question: Explain the Strategy pattern. Contrast it with the Factory pattern.
idealAnswer: Strategy is a behavioral pattern that lets you define a family of algorithms, put each into a separate class, and make their objects interchangeable dynamically at runtime. Example: A ShoppingCart has a setPaymentStrategy(IPaymentStrategy p) method. You can swap between BkashStrategy and CardStrategy dynamically. Contrast: Factory is about CREATING objects. Strategy is about BEHAVIOR — doing a task dynamically in different ways using polymorphism.

question: Explain the Observer pattern. Where is it used?
idealAnswer: Observer is a behavioral pattern where one subject notifies multiple observer objects automatically of any state changes. Example: Notification system. A User registers an event (Subject). EmailNotifier, SMSNotifier, and PushNotifier (Observers) are subscribed. Once the event happens, the Subject calls notify() and all Observers execute their trigger. Heavily used in Event Listeners, UI interactions, and Pub/Sub mechanics.

question: Design a URL Shortener system using OOP. Specify classes and the flow.
idealAnswer: Problem: Convert long URL to short URL. Classes: 1) URLService: Core logic coordinator. 2) URLRepository (Interface): Handles DB operations (save, retrieve). 3) ShortCodeGenerator: Encapsulates the algorithmic logic (Base62 encoding or hashing). Flow: User calls URLService with longURL. URLService asks ShortCodeGenerator for short sequence. URLService passes sequence + longURL to URLRepository to store in DB. Returns Short URL. OOP used: Encapsulation (hiding hash logic), SRP (generator separates logic from DB ops), DIP (Service relies on Repository Interface for easy DB swapping).

question: Design a Payment System supporting multiple methods (bKash, Card, Nagad) using OOP.
idealAnswer: Using the Strategy Pattern. Components: 1) PaymentProcessor (Context class). 2) IPaymentStrategy (Interface with method pay(amount)). 3) BkashPayment, CardPayment, NagadPayment (Concrete Strategies implementing IPaymentStrategy). Flow: Client selects method. System sets the IPaymentStrategy in PaymentProcessor. Calls executePayment(). Benefit: Adding a new payment method requires ZERO changes to existing classes, fully adhering to Open/Closed Principle (OCP) and leveraging Runtime Polymorphism.

question: Design an OOP solution for calculating the Area of a Square and Rectangle without duplicating code and making it scalable for new shapes.
idealAnswer: Use Inheritance and Polymorphism. Classes: 1) Shape (Abstract Base Class) with pure virtual method calculateArea() = 0. 2) Square inherits Shape, takes side_length in constructor, implements calculateArea() returning side*side. 3) Rectangle inherits Shape, takes length and width, implements calculateArea() returning length*width. Benefit: We can hold a List of Shape pointers and loop through them calling calculateArea(), seamlessly supporting any future shapes.

question: Design a Notification System using OOP that can easily scale to handle new notification types.
idealAnswer: Use interfaces to follow Open/Closed Principle. Components: 1) INotifier (Interface with method send(message, user)). 2) Content Classes: EmailNotifier, SMSNotifier, PushNotifier implementing INotifier. 3) NotificationService: Receives the user, message, and a specific INotifier instance (Dependency Injection). Benefit: If a new notification channel is needed (e.g., WhatsAppNotifier), just implement INotifier. The NotificationService code does not change.

question: How does a program run? Explain the flow from source code to execution.
idealAnswer: 1) Preprocessing: Handles directives like #include and macros. 2) Compilation: The compiler converts the human-readable source code into assembly code. 3) Assembly: The assembler converts assembly code into machine code (object files, mostly binary 0s and 1s). 4) Linking: The linker combines the object file with necessary libraries into a single Executable file. 5) Loading: When executed, the OS Loader places the executable into RAM (memory). 6) Execution: The CPU fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and executes them step-by-step.
keyPoints: Code -> Compiler -> Assembly -> Binary Object -> Linker -> Executable -> RAM -> CPU

question: How do you structure code in an enterprise OOP system?
idealAnswer: Enterprise code is structured using layered architecture to enforce Separation of Concerns. Layers: 1) Presentation/Controller Layer: Handles HTTP requests, takes inputs. No business logic. 2) Service/Business Layer: Contains core logic, validations, and rules. 3) Data Access/Repository Layer: Strictly handles talking to the database or external APIs. 4) Models/Entities: Plain objects holding state. This ensures components are loosely coupled, testable, and adhere to SRP.

question: Difference between Coupling and Cohesion. What do we strive for?
idealAnswer: Coupling refers to how heavily components depend on each other. High coupling means a change in Class A breaks Class B. Cohesion refers to how strongly related the inside parts of a Class are. High cohesion means a class does exactly one highly-focused job well. Goal: Low Coupling (independent modules) and High Cohesion (focused modules).

question: Can constructors be overloaded? Can they be overridden?
idealAnswer: Yes, constructors CAN be overloaded. You can have multiple constructors with different parameter lists (e.g., a default constructor taking 0 args, and a parameterized constructor taking 2 args). No, constructors CANNOT be overridden. Overriding requires inheritance, and child classes cannot inherit constructors in the typical method sense—they call parent constructors, but cannot redefine the parent's constructor.

question: How is memory allocated for Objects vs Primitives?
idealAnswer: Primitives (int, char, double) are typically allocated on the Stack, which is fast and automatically cleaned up when scope ends. Objects, especially those created via 'new' keyword (in C++/Java), are allocated on the Heap. The Heap is dynamic and larger, but slower to access. The object reference (pointer) is stored on the Stack, pointing to the bulk data on the Heap.
keyPoints: Pointer to object kept on Stack

question: What is Late binding vs Early binding?
idealAnswer: Early Binding (Static Binding): Resolved at compile time. Used by overloaded methods and normal non-virtual functions. The compiler directly hooks the execution address. Late Binding (Dynamic Binding): Resolved at runtime. Used by overridden virtual methods via a vtable structure. Slower than early binding but necessary for runtime polymorphism.

question: Why do we use an interface instead of an abstract class in Dependency Injection?
idealAnswer: Interfaces define a strict contract with ZERO implementation. When passing dependencies (like an IPaymentProcessor into a CheckoutService), we don't care how the payment works, just that it fulfills the interface rules. Abstract classes sometimes carry hidden states or logic that tightens coupling. Interfaces ensure the purest form of loose coupling and make unit testing with Mock objects incredibly easy.

question: How does Polymorphism help reduce cyclomatic complexity (reducing if/else statements)?
idealAnswer: Instead of writing huge switch statements or if/else chains to determine object behavior (e.g., if type == 'Car' do X, else if type == 'Bike' do Y), polymorphism allows you to define an abstract method (like move()) and have Car and Bike implement it individually. The caller simply calls animal->move() or object->move() without checking its specific type, eliminating the complex conditional branch.

question: What happens if we don’t use Encapsulation?
idealAnswer: Without Encapsulation (i.e. having public variables everywhere), external classes can alter an object's state invalidly (e.g., setting age to -5). It makes tracking state changes impossible, creating debugging nightmares. A change in internal representation (changing a variable type) will immediately break all classes relying on it. Getters/Setters protect rules and provide a stable API.

question: In Java/C#, what is the difference between an Exception and an Error?
idealAnswer: Error: Critical conditions typically occurring at the system or JVM level (e.g., OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError). Applications should not try to catch these as recovery is usually impossible. Exception: Conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch and recover from (e.g., FileNotFoundException, NullReferenceException). Handled via try/catch blocks.
keyPoints: Exception = App level, recoverable via catch
